SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper provides experimental evidence supporting its main claims (e.g., CNN variants outperform traditional systems by up to 11.27%, Section 6.2) and describes datasets, training regime and hyperparameters. However, mathematical derivations are absent and some methodological details are underspecified (no description of significance tests despite stating an improvement is "statistically insignificant" and limited discussion of random seeds or variance). The argument that CNNs will learn meaningful gaze filters (Section 3) is plausible but largely speculative and not strongly validated beyond small empirical gains, leaving some unsupported assumptions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The work applies CNNs to jointly learn representations from gaze and text and frames this as automatic extraction of cognitive features rather than manual feature engineering, which is a clear contribution claimed in the abstract and Section 4. This is an incremental extension of existing CNN-based text models to an additional modality (gaze) rather than a fundamentally new model class or learning paradigm. The paper differentiates itself from prior handcrafted cognitive-feature approaches (cites Mishra et al./Joshi et al.), but the core technique (CNN on multimodal inputs) is a moderate novelty.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized with a clear pipeline (motivation, CNN architecture, experiment setup, results) and explicitly lists datasets, CNN variants, hyperparameters and training details (Section 5). Figures and component references (e.g., Figure 2 and Figure 3) are used to explain ideas, though those figures are not included in the provided excerpt; some claims (e.g., the analogy between image filters and gaze filters) are stated without full elaboration. Overall the presentation is readable with minor omissions and occasional speculative language.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments cover two datasets with multiple CNN variants (text-only, gaze-only, and combined) and follow prior 10-fold train–test configurations (Section 5.7), showing empirical evaluation across tasks. Important analyses are missing or limited: there is little discussion of ablations or sensitivity (they note hyperparameters were set by trial-and-error and that tuning is future work), limited reporting of statistical testing details, and scant discussion of failure cases or when gaze helps. Reproducibility is partially supported by hyperparameter descriptions but lacks full architectural specifics.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses a relevant problem — leveraging cognitive (gaze) signals for sentiment and sarcasm detection — which could interest researchers in cognitive NLP and multimodal learning. Results show modest gains and that text often dominates (e.g., gaze adds only a 1.36% improvement for sarcasm, Section 6.2), so the work is unlikely to be transformative but may prompt further study of learned cognitive features. The contribution could be useful to a niche community exploring behavioral signals for NLP.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (multimodal cognitive features for NLP classification) and the methodological approach (CNN-based representation learning, experimental comparison to prior work) fit well within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences. The paper uses standard evaluation practices for the field (10-fold splits, comparison to cited baselines) and addresses the right audience, though stronger experimental rigor would strengthen venue suitability.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The authors compare against existing handcrafted-feature systems (Mishra et al., Joshi et al.) and state they follow the same 10-fold train–test configuration for consistency (Section 5.7), which is appropriate. However, the paper gives limited detail on which and how many baselines were included, lacks full reporting of statistical tests used to assess significance, and offers limited analysis of when and why the proposed method outperforms or fails relative to baselines (the small 1.36% gaze improvement is noted but not deeply analyzed).
